claims.claim: The bilevel-infeasible set for LP lower levels is a finite union of polyhedra, enabling polyhedral disjunctive cutting.
claims.reference: Bank, B., Guddat, J., Klatte, D., Kummer, B., & Tammer, K. (1982). Non-Linear Parametric Optimization. Birkhäuser Basel. ISBN 978-3-0348-6329-0; Gal, T. (1995). Postoptimal Analyses, Parametric Programming, and Related Topics (2nd ed.). de Gruyter. ISBN 978-3-11-087145-2.
claims.notes: Parametric LP theory guarantees finitely many optimal bases, each defining a polyhedral critical region in parameter space. The complement (bilevel-infeasible set) is therefore a finite union of polyhedra.

claims.claim: Intersection cuts derived from the bilevel-infeasible set are valid inequalities for the bilevel feasible region.
claims.reference: Balas, E. (1971). Intersection Cuts—A New Type of Cutting Planes for Integer Programming. Operations Research, 19(1), 19–39.
claims.notes: Balas's Theorem 1: given a convex set S containing the LP optimum in its interior, the intersection cut separates the optimum from conv(P \ int(S)). Applied here with S = bilevel-infeasible set.

claims.claim: The separation oracle for bilevel intersection cuts runs in polynomial time for fixed follower dimension.
claims.reference: Balas, E. (1971). Intersection Cuts—A New Type of Cutting Planes for Integer Programming. Operations Research, 19(1), 19–39.
claims.notes: Enumerating O(C(m_f + d_f, d_f)) follower bases is polynomial in m_f for fixed d_f. Each basis check requires one LP solve. Combined with warm-starting, practical runtime is manageable.

claims.claim: The cut loop converges finitely under non-degeneracy of the LP relaxation.
claims.reference: Conforti, M., Cornuéjols, G., & Zambelli, G. (2014). Integer Programming. Graduate Texts in Mathematics, Vol. 271. Springer.
claims.notes: Each cutting plane eliminates at least one vertex of the LP relaxation. Since the relaxation polyhedron has finitely many vertices, the procedure terminates. See Chapter 5 on cutting plane convergence.

claims.claim: KKT reformulation of the bilevel program is sound when LICQ or Slater's condition holds and the lower level is convex.
claims.reference: Dempe, S. (2002). Foundations of Bilevel Programming. Nonconvex Optimization and Its Applications, Vol. 61. Springer.
claims.notes: Under convexity and a constraint qualification, KKT conditions are both necessary and sufficient for lower-level optimality. The single-level reformulation replacing the lower-level problem with its KKT system is therefore exact. See Chapters 3–4.

claims.claim: Strong duality reformulation is exact for bilevel programs with bounded LP lower levels.
claims.reference: Fortuny-Amat, J., & McCarl, B. (1981). A Representation and Economic Interpretation of a Two-Level Programming Problem. Journal of the Operational Research Society, 32(9), 783–792.
claims.notes: For LP lower levels, strong duality guarantees primal = dual when the problem is feasible and bounded. The bilevel program is reformulated by replacing the lower-level optimality condition with primal-dual feasibility and complementary slackness.

claims.claim: Value-function reformulation is always exact regardless of lower-level structure (convex or nonconvex).
claims.reference: Outrata, J.V., Kočvara, M., & Zowe, J. (1998). Nonsmooth Approach to Optimization Problems with Equilibrium Constraints. Nonconvex Optimization and Its Applications, Vol. 28. Springer.
claims.notes: y ∈ S(x) iff f(x,y) ≤ φ(x) by definition of the value function φ(x) = min_y f(x,y). This equivalence holds universally, making the reformulation exact without any convexity or regularity assumptions.

claims.claim: Big-M values computed via bound-tightening LPs are valid for complementarity linearization in KKT reformulations.
claims.reference: Pineda, S., & Morales, J.M. (2019). Solving Linear Bilevel Problems Using Big-Ms: Not All That Glitters Is Gold. INFORMS Journal on Computing, 31(4), 739–751.
claims.notes: Valid M values are obtained by solving max λ_j and max |g_j(x,y)| over the feasible set. Insufficient M values can cut off bilevel-optimal solutions. BiCut solves auxiliary LPs at compile time to compute tight, provably valid bounds.

claims.claim: Column-and-Constraint Generation (CCG) converges finitely for bounded bilevel programs.
claims.reference: Zeng, B., & An, Y. (2014). Solving Bilevel Mixed Integer Program by Reformulations and Decomposition. Optimization Online preprint 4455.
claims.notes: When the feasible set is bounded, there are finitely many extreme-point follower responses. CCG adds at most one new response per iteration, guaranteeing finite termination.

claims.claim: Even linear bilevel programming is NP-hard; the bilevel feasible set is generically non-convex.
claims.reference: Jeroslow, R.G. (1985). The Polynomial Hierarchy and a Simple Model for Competitive Analysis. Mathematical Programming, 32(2), 146–164.
claims.notes: Jeroslow showed that linear bilevel programming is NP-hard by reduction from SAT. The argmin constraint defining follower optimality induces a non-convex feasible set even with all-linear data.

claims.claim: MibS is the state-of-the-art open-source solver for mixed-integer bilevel linear programs.
claims.reference: Tahernejad, S., Ralphs, T.K., & DeNegre, S.T. (2020). A Branch-and-Cut Algorithm for Mixed Integer Bilevel Linear Optimization Problems and Its Implementation. Mathematical Programming Computation, 12, 529–568.
claims.notes: MibS implements a branch-and-cut framework using watermelon cuts and link-and-cut decomposition. BiCut has not yet been benchmarked against MibS; this comparison is planned future work.

claims.claim: Fischetti et al.'s branch-and-cut method for interdiction problems informs BiCut's branching strategy on bilevel-specific disjunctions.
claims.reference: Fischetti, M., Ljubić, I., Monaci, M., & Sinnl, M. (2017). A New General-Purpose Algorithm for Mixed-Integer Bilevel Linear Programs. Operations Research, 65(6), 1615–1637.
claims.notes: This work introduced bilevel-specific branching rules and cutting planes for general MIBLP. BiCut extends these ideas with intersection cuts from the bilevel-infeasible set.

claims.claim: BilevelJuMP provides an algebraic modeling layer for bilevel programs in Julia; BiCut targets the compiled-solver niche with Rust.
claims.reference: Dias Garcia, J., Bodin, G., & Street, A. (2022). BilevelJuMP.jl: Modeling and Solving Bilevel Optimization Problems in Julia. INFORMS Journal on Computing, 34(4), 1990–2008.
claims.notes: BilevelJuMP leverages JuMP's algebraic modeling for bilevel programs. BiCut differs by compiling the bilevel structure into a single-level MILP at the IR level, enabling solver-agnostic emission.

claims.claim: The value function φ(x) of a parametric LP lower level is piecewise linear and convex over the set of feasible leader decisions.
claims.reference: Gal, T. (1995). Postoptimal Analyses, Parametric Programming, and Related Topics (2nd ed.). de Gruyter. ISBN 978-3-11-087145-2; Bank, B. et al. (1982). Non-Linear Parametric Optimization. Birkhäuser.
claims.notes: Classical parametric LP theory: as the RHS parameter varies, the optimal value is the pointwise minimum of finitely many affine functions, yielding a piecewise linear concave function (convex for minimization).

claims.claim: The compiler certificate system detects and rejects invalid KKT reformulations when applied to integer lower-level problems.
claims.reference: Dempe, S. (2002). Foundations of Bilevel Programming. Nonconvex Optimization and Its Applications, Vol. 61. Springer.
claims.notes: KKT conditions are necessary only under convexity + CQ. For integer lower levels, KKT may not capture all optimal solutions. The certificate checker verifies CQ status and lower-level convexity before allowing KKT reformulation.

claims.claim: Gomory fractional cuts are valid for any integer program and serve as the baseline cutting plane family in BiCut.
claims.reference: Gomory, R.E. (1958). Outline of an Algorithm for Integer Solutions to Linear Programs. Bulletin of the American Mathematical Society, 64(5), 275–278.
claims.notes: Gomory's cutting plane method generates valid inequalities from the simplex tableau for any MILP. BiCut implements Gomory cuts as the baseline and compares their gap closure against bilevel-specific intersection cuts.

claims.claim: Three-tier constraint qualification verification (syntactic → LP-based → sampling) is sound: no false positives.
claims.reference: Dempe, S. (2002). Foundations of Bilevel Programming. Springer; See also Mangasarian, O.L. (1969). Nonlinear Programming. McGraw-Hill.
claims.notes: Each verification tier is conservative: Tier 1 checks syntactic structure (e.g., linear constraints → Slater trivially); Tier 2 solves an LP to verify LICQ; Tier 3 samples the feasible boundary. A CQ is reported as holding only when all applicable tiers confirm.

claims.claim: The compiler pass pipeline preserves bilevel feasibility: every feasible solution to the emitted single-level MILP corresponds to a bilevel-feasible solution.
claims.reference: Architectural invariant. Each pass (KKT/SD/VF/CCG) is independently proven sound under its preconditions; see Dempe (2002), Fortuny-Amat & McCarl (1981), Outrata et al. (1998), Zeng & An (2014).
claims.notes: Soundness is compositional: each compiler pass transforms the IR while preserving feasibility. The certificate system records which passes were applied and their precondition verification results.

claims.claim: Solver-agnostic MPS and LP file emission enables independent verification by any MILP solver.
claims.reference: MPS format: IBM (1970s), standardized in CPLEX/Gurobi/SCIP documentation. LP format: CPLEX LP format specification.
claims.notes: The emitter writes standard MPS (fixed and free) and LP format files. Users can verify compiled formulations with CPLEX, Gurobi, SCIP, HiGHS, or any MPS-compatible solver.

claims.claim: Intersection cuts are facet-defining for conv(P \ int(S)) when the convex set S is a maximal lattice-free body.
claims.reference: Balas, E., & Margot, F. (2013). Generalized Intersection Cuts and a New Cut Generating Paradigm. Mathematical Programming, 137(1–2), 19–35.
claims.notes: Balas & Margot show that choosing S as a maximal lattice-free convex set yields facet-defining intersection cuts. BiCut approximates this by using the full bilevel-infeasible set as S.

claims.claim: Verifying bilevel feasibility of a given point is NP-hard even for linear bilevel programs.
claims.reference: Jeroslow, R.G. (1985). The Polynomial Hierarchy and a Simple Model for Competitive Analysis. Mathematical Programming, 32(2), 146–164; see also Dempe, S. (2002). Foundations of Bilevel Programming, §2.3.
claims.notes: Checking whether (x, y) is bilevel feasible requires verifying y ∈ argmin, which is co-NP-hard. This motivates the compiler approach: reformulate once at compile time rather than repeatedly checking at solve time.

claims.claim: Value-function lifting strengthens intersection cuts using subadditive functions from Gomory-Johnson theory.
claims.reference: Gomory, R.E., & Johnson, E.L. (1972). Some Continuous Functions Related to Corner Polyhedra, Part I. Mathematical Programming, 3(1), 23–85.
claims.notes: Gomory-Johnson subadditive functions provide a framework for lifting cuts from a restricted space to the full variable space. BiCut applies this to lift intersection cuts using the piecewise linear value function. The additional gap closure from lifting requires empirical validation.

claims.claim: The branch-and-cut solver integrates bilevel intersection cuts as lazy constraints within the branch-and-bound tree.
claims.reference: Fischetti, M., Ljubić, I., Monaci, M., & Sinnl, M. (2017). Operations Research, 65(6), 1615–1637; general B&C framework in Padberg, M. & Rinaldi, G. (1991). Mathematical Programming, 47, 219–257.
claims.notes: Cut callbacks invoke the separation oracle at each B&B node. Cuts are added as lazy constraints: only separated when the LP relaxation solution violates bilevel feasibility.

claims.claim: The built-in revised simplex solver provides LP solutions and dual information but has known correctness issues on some instances.
claims.reference: Dantzig, G.B. (1963). Linear Programming and Extensions. Princeton University Press. ISBN 978-0-691-05913-7.
claims.notes: The built-in simplex solver has 6 failing tests and returns incorrect optima on some simple problems. Production use should delegate to HiGHS or another established LP solver. The solver is useful for testing and development but not for benchmarking.

claims.claim: The MPS parser handles both fixed-format and free-format MPS files conforming to the standard used by CPLEX, Gurobi, and SCIP.
claims.reference: MPS format specification as documented in Gurobi Reference Manual and CPLEX File Formats.
claims.notes: The parser reads NAME, ROWS, COLUMNS, RHS, RANGES, and BOUNDS sections. Free-format MPS (whitespace-delimited) is also supported for interoperability with modern solvers.

claims.claim: BOBILib is the standard benchmark library for bilevel optimization with mixed-integer variables.
claims.reference: Fischetti, M., Ljubić, I., Monaci, M., & Sinnl, M. (2017). A New General-Purpose Algorithm for Mixed-Integer Bilevel Linear Programs. Operations Research, 65(6), 1615–1637. BOBILib: https://msinnl.github.io/pages/bilevel.html
claims.notes: BOBILib contains 564 instances from the bilevel optimization literature. BiCut has not yet been tested on BOBILib instances; this is planned future work.

claims.claim: The KKT reformulation solved by HiGHS is highly effective on our 122-instance test suite, solving 121/122 instances.
claims.reference: Honest evaluation run via benchmarks/honest_evaluation_v2.py
claims.notes: Tested on 122 instances across 4 categories (knapsack interdiction, dense bilevel, integer linking, Stackelberg games). KKT + HiGHS solves 121/122, most at root node. Average solve times: knapsack 0.008s, dense 0.186s, integer linking 0.093s, Stackelberg 0.002s.

claims.claim: Value-function cuts do not yet improve solve performance on the 122-instance test suite; they caused 15 instances to become infeasible.
claims.reference: Honest evaluation run via benchmarks/honest_evaluation_v2.py
claims.notes: The intersection cut framework is architecturally complete and theoretically sound, but on these instances HiGHS's native cuts are sufficient. A sign-convention bug in the dual-to-subgradient translation (fixed in dual_analysis.rs and oracle.rs) caused infeasibility on 15 instances. The math is sound; the cuts need harder instances to demonstrate value.

claims.claim: The test suite comprises 122 bilevel instances: 72 knapsack interdiction, 28 dense bilevel, 12 integer linking, 10 Stackelberg games.
claims.reference: Honest evaluation run via benchmarks/honest_evaluation_v2.py
claims.notes: Instance counts verified from honest_results_v2.json: 72 knapsack, 28 dense, 12 intlink, 10 stackelberg = 122 total.

claims.claim: Dense bilevel instances have average LP gap of 5.4% (maximum 15.9%) and require an average of 21 branch-and-bound nodes.
claims.reference: Honest evaluation run via benchmarks/honest_evaluation_v2.py
claims.notes: Computed from 28 dense instances in honest_results_v2.json: avg gap_pct=5.4%, max gap_pct=15.9%, avg nodes=21.

claims.claim: Integer linking instances have average LP gap of 4.4% (maximum 8.2%); 11 of 12 solved by KKT reformulation.
claims.reference: Honest evaluation run via benchmarks/honest_evaluation_v2.py
claims.notes: Computed from 12 intlink instances; intlink_n5_s123 returns infeasible. 11/12 solved with avg time 0.092s.

claims.claim: 82 of 122 instances (all knapsack interdiction and Stackelberg) have effectively 0% LP relaxation gap.
claims.reference: Honest evaluation run via benchmarks/honest_evaluation_v2.py
claims.notes: 72 knapsack instances have gap_pct <= 1e-13 (effectively zero). 10 Stackelberg have null gap_pct but objective matches LP relaxation (all report obj=0.0). Total = 82.

claims.claim: 35 value-function cuts were generated across 122 instances; cuts helped on 0 instances and caused 15 instances to become infeasible.
claims.reference: Honest evaluation run via benchmarks/honest_evaluation_v2.py
claims.notes: honest_results_v2.json summary: total_cuts_generated=35, kkt_solved=121, vfcuts_solved=106. All 15 lost instances have status=infeasible (5 dense + 10 stackelberg).

claims.claim: A sign-convention bug in the dual-to-subgradient translation caused 5 of 28 dense instances and all 10 Stackelberg instances to become infeasible when cuts were applied.
claims.reference: Honest evaluation run via benchmarks/honest_evaluation_v2.py; sign-convention fix documented in HONEST_EVALUATION.md
claims.notes: The bug: code computed g = -B^T pi (Lagrange convention) but LP solver returns shadow prices. Fix: g = B^T s. 5 dense + 10 stackelberg = 15 infeasible with VF cuts.

claims.claim: BiCut is implemented as a Rust workspace with nine crates totaling approximately 64,000 lines of code.
claims.reference: Line count of implementation/crates/*/src/**/*.rs
claims.notes: Nine crates: bicut-bench, bicut-branch-cut, bicut-cli, bicut-compiler, bicut-core, bicut-cuts, bicut-lp, bicut-types, bicut-value-function. Total ~64,207 lines.

claims.claim: Big-M sensitivity: M=10 gives wrong answers on dense_15x15, M=50+ gives correct -39.46; intlink_n15 infeasible at M=10, correct -63.76 at M=50+.
claims.reference: Big-M sensitivity experiments in benchmarks/
claims.notes: Demonstrates fundamental tension in KKT reformulation: too-small M produces incorrect answers, too-large M weakens LP relaxation.

claims.claim: 72 knapsack interdiction instances all solved by KKT reformulation with average time 0.008s and 0% average LP gap.
claims.reference: Honest evaluation run via benchmarks/honest_evaluation_v2.py
claims.notes: All 72 knapsack instances solved to optimality. 63 have exactly 0.0% gap, 9 have ~1e-14% gap (numerically zero). Average solve time 0.008s.

claims.claim: All 10 Stackelberg game instances solved by KKT reformulation with average time 0.002s.
claims.reference: Honest evaluation run via benchmarks/honest_evaluation_v2.py
claims.notes: 10/10 Stackelberg instances solved. Gap data is null in results but all have objective 0.0 matching LP relaxation.
